items.title: Radial fracture in Galeazzi injury
items.contentHtml:
Middle distal third radius fracture

  • Galeazzi injury: fracture of the radial shaft in the middle or distal third associated with dislocation of the distal radioulnar joint (DRA).
  • The fracture is usually short oblique or transverse, with angulation and shortening of the radius, which causes instability of the DRA.
  • Classification of Rettig: type I (distal third), type II (middle third), type III (proximal third).
  • Differential diagnosis: Monteggia fracture (proximal ulna fracture with radial head dislocation).
  • In adults, conservative treatment (cast) fails due to rotational instability; surgical fixation with plate and screws is mandatory.

items.title: Distal radioulnar dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Distal radioulnar joint dislocation

  • The dislocation of the distal radioulnar joint (DRA) can be dorsal (more common) or volar, determined by the displacement of the ulna with respect to the radius.
  • In Galeazzi's injury, the dislocation is dorsal in most cases, associated with forced pronation.
  • Key radiological sign: In anteroposterior radiograph, the ulna appears superimposed on the radius; in profile, the ulna is displaced dorsally.
  • Closed reduction of the dislocation is performed after stable fixation of the radial fracture; if instability persists, temporary fixation with Kirschner wires is required.
  • Residual instability of ARCD is the main complication if the radial length is not corrected.

items.title: Fall with outstretched hand
items.contentHtml:
Fall on outstretched hand mechanism

  • The fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH: fall on outstretched hand) transmits an axial load to the distal radius, producing a diaphyseal fracture if the forearm is pronated.
  • The force is transmitted through the carpus to the articular surface of the radius; the ulna acts as a fixed point, generating dislocation of the ARCD.
  • This mechanism also produces fractures of the distal radius (Colles, Smith, Barton) but in the Galeazzi injury the fracture is diaphyseal, not metaphyseal.
  • The pronation of the forearm at the moment of impact increases the tension on the dorsal radioulnar ligament and the triangular fibrocartilage complex (CFTC).
  • Diagnosis differential with Goyrand-Smith fracture (volar) or Colles fracture (dorsal) due to metaphyseal location.

items.title: forced pronation
items.contentHtml:
Forced pronation of the forearm

  • Forced pronation of the forearm is the typical mechanism of Galeazzi injury, common in throwing sports or falls with rotation.
  • During extreme pronation, the radius crosses over the ulna, straining the dorsal radioulnar ligament and the TFCC, which dislocates the ARCD.
  • The radial fracture is caused by the combination of axial loading and rotation, generating a short oblique line in the middle-distal third.
  • Reduction of the dislocation requires supination of the forearm and longitudinal traction after fixing the radial fracture.
  • Immobilization in postoperative supination protects the reduction of the ARCD.

items.title: Surgical fixation
items.contentHtml:
Surgical fixation of the fracture

  • Surgical fixation of the radial fracture in Galeazzi injury is mandatory in adults; Conservative treatment produces 80-90% poor results.
  • It is performed with plate and screws dynamic compression (DCP) or locking (LCP) through Henry's anterior approach (between the brachioradialis and the flexor carpi radialis).
  • Objective: restore radial length, alignment and rotation, allowing spontaneous reduction of ARCD dislocation.
  • At least 6 cortices are required. of fixation proximal and distal to the fracture (bicortical screws).
  • In comminuted fractures, autologous bone graft or substitute can be added to prevent collapse.

items.title: Closed reduction of dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Closed reduction of dislocation

  • Closed reduction of dislocation of ARCD is attempted after stable radial osteosynthesis; If the fracture is well aligned, the dislocation usually reduces spontaneously.
  • Maneuver: supination of the forearm and direct pressure on the head of the ulna in a volar direction (if the dislocation is dorsal).
  • If the dislocation persists after fixation, the radial length should be checked; If correct, a percutaneous Kirschner wire is placed, transfixing the ARCD in supination for 4-6 weeks.
  • Open reduction is rare and is reserved for irreducible dislocations due to interposition of the CFCT or the extensor carpi ulnaris.
  • Residual instability is evaluated with intraoperative dynamic fluoroscopy.

items.title: Distal radioulnar instability
items.contentHtml:
Distal radioulnar instability

  • Instability of the distal radioulnar joint (DRA) is the most common complication after a poorly treated Galeazzi injury.
  • It is caused by residual radial shortening greater than 5 mm or by angulation of the uncorrected radial fracture.
  • Clinically it manifests itself with pain on the ulnar side of the wrist, clicking and sensation of instability during pronosupination.
  • Radioulnar box test: anteroposterior displacement of the ulna with respect to the radius greater than 5 mm suggests instability.
  • Surgical treatment includes reconstruction of the CFCT or arthrodesis of the ARCD (Darrach or Sauvé-Kapandji procedure).

items.title: Posterior interosseous nerve injury
items.contentHtml:
Injury of the posterior interosseous nerve

  • The injury of the posterior interosseous nerve (motor branch of the radial nerve) is a rare complication but described in the Galeazzi injury.
  • It occurs due to entrapment between the fragments of the radial fracture or due to compression during surgical reduction.
  • Clinically it produces a deficit in finger extension (dropping of the thumb and fingers long) without dorsal sensory loss of the hand (preservation of the superficial radial nerve).
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with electromyography and nerve conduction study after 3-4 weeks if there is no recovery.
  • Surgical exploration and neurolysis is indicated if there is no improvement after 4-6 months of observation.

items.title: Plate and screws
items.contentHtml:
Plate and screw fixation

  • Plate and screw fixation is the gold standard for radial fracture in Galeazzi injury.
  • A 3.5 mm dynamic compression plate (DCP) or a locking plate (LCP) is used for metaphyseal or comminuted fractures.
  • The plate is placed on the volar aspect of the radius (Henry approach) to avoid irritation of the extensor tendons.
  • At least 3 bicortical screws are required proximal and 3 distal to the fracture, with interfragmentary compression if the line is oblique.
  • Fixation with intramedullary nails is not indicated due to rotational instability.

items.title: Conservative contraindication
items.contentHtml:
No conservative treatment in adults

  • Conservative treatment (brachiopalmar cast) in adults with Galeazzi lesion is contraindicated due to the high failure rate (up to 90% of malunion or nonunion).
  • The instability of ARCD is not corrected with external immobilization due to the action of the pronator quadratus and brachioradialis muscles, which shorten and angulate the radius.
  • In children, conservative treatment may be acceptable if the fracture is not displaced and the dislocation is reduced, but in adults surgical fixation is mandatory.
  • The exception would be patients with absolute surgical contraindication (severe comorbidity), in whom closed reduction and supination casting are attempted, with weekly radiological monitoring.
  • Radial nonunion is the most serious consequence of conservative treatment.

items.title: Association with ulna fracture
items.contentHtml:
Association with fracture of the ulna

  • The association with fracture of the ulna in the same forearm is called equivalent Galeazzi fracture or atypical Galeazzi lesion.
  • The ulnar fracture is usually in the distal third or in the styloid process of the ulna, and the dislocation may be of the ARCD or radial head (mixed form).
  • The mechanism is similar: fall with an outstretched hand and forced pronation, but with greater energy.
  • Treatment requires fixation of both fractures with plate and screws, and evaluation of the stability of the ARCD.
  • The prognosis is worse than in the classic Galeazzi injury, with a greater risk of stiffness and post-traumatic osteoarthritis.

items.title: Radial pseudoarthrosis
items.contentHtml:
Complication: radial pseudoarthrosis

  • Radial pseudoarthrosis is a serious complication of the Galeazzi injury, with an incidence of 5-10% in fractures treated surgically and higher in conservative treatments.
  • Risk factors: comminuted fracture, infection, instability of the uncorrected ARCD, smoking, vitamin deficiency D.
  • Clinically: pain at the fracture site, abnormal mobility and angular deformity; The x-ray shows the absence of bone callus and sclerosis of the edges.
  • Surgical treatment consists of resection of the fibrous tissue, reaming of the ends, autologous bone graft from the iliac crest and new osteosynthesis with a compression plate.
  • Untreated radial nonunion leads to progressive radial shortening and chronic instability of the ARCD.
